title: Working with a Financial Advisor or Elder Law Attorney
summary: Knowing which professional to call — and when — saves families real time and money during an already stressful period.
category: Life Transitions III: Legal & Financial Shifts
author: HarborCare Circle Editorial
seoTitle: Working with a Financial Advisor or Elder Law Attorney | HarborCare Circle
metaDescription: Knowing which professional to call — and when — saves families real time and money during an already stressful period.
whatYouNeedToKnow: Elder law attorneys handle legal documents, Medicaid planning, and asset protection.
Financial advisors handle investment, income, and broader financial planning.
Some situations need both, working together.

paragraphs: Elder law attorneys are the right call for legal documents (POA, wills, trusts), Medicaid planning, and guardianship matters — areas requiring legal expertise specific to aging and long-term care. Financial advisors focus more on investment strategy, income planning, and the broader financial picture, including retirement and insurance decisions.

Complex situations often benefit from both professionals working together, ideally coordinating with each other directly, rather than a family relaying information back and forth between two separate conversations.

nextSteps: Identify which type of professional matches your immediate need.
Ask directly about elder-care experience specifically, not just general practice.
Consider bringing both professionals together for complex situations.
disclaimer: General guidance for choosing the right professional — this article itself isn't a substitute for either kind of professional advice.

sources.name: Getting Your Affairs in Order Checklist
